text: Global tech leaders arrived in Delhi, took the stage and spoke of India as the future. Microsoft, Google and Nvidia praised its scale and potential. But you know what? Beyond the spotlight, companies in Bengaluru were building. The story is definitely changing from market to maker. And now of course I would like to introduce you to Indis which serverai has unveiled and it describes it as a fully integrated system built end to end rather than lay onto existing platforms. The company says it has developed everything from core infrastructure to the interface. At its center is Servam 105B a large model built in India alongside a lighter 30B version. Both it says are designed to operate domestically without relying on overseas servers. This is however a complication. Global models are polished and precise but they can of course feel slightly out of place. Ask one to decode a message like for example bro work from home but to scene and of course you may get a literal explanation about remote work anduling constraints. What it actually misses is the subtext which is well I'm technically online but don't expect any miracles of me. Indis by contrast is designed with that linguistic shift in mind. The company says it supports more than 22 Indian languages natively and is built to operate comfortably across code switching conversations. In a country where many users come online through voice rather than text, that nuance could prove very significant. The numbers are striking. Around a 100 million people in India use chat GPD each week and the country is one of the biggest markets for claude. Activity on that scale plays a part in how these systems are improved and developed over time. Server however is positioning itself more broadly. The company is not limiting its ambitions to a chatbot but outlining plans for a wider technology ecosystem. It has announced partnerships with HMD for Nokia feature phones, collaborations with Bosch in the automotive space, and optimization work with Qualcomm to improve ondevice performance. They even teased Kaz smart glasses, AI you wear and AI you live. But then of course the question arises, is Servam a lone wolf? Not at all. The India AI mission is a 10,000 K rupees corpus facilitating well BA geni gen Bajen which has just launched well PAM 2 a 17B model capturing dialects the west doesn't even know exist then of course we have Yani.ai AI which has announced Vajana voice cloning for low bandwidth rural areas. Also there is tech Mahindra which is training an 8B Hindi first model for education. This is a very well-coordinated effort. The goal AI assisted governance and health care for the last mile and not just for the tech community in for example Indranagar. Then of course there is Kutim. Its founder Bhavish Agarval set out to build chips cloud and models ideally all at once. It was an admirably large promise. The follow through has involved well

care for the last mile and not just for the tech community in for example Indranagar. Then of course there is Kutim. Its founder Bhavish Agarval set out to build chips cloud and models ideally all at once. It was an admirably large promise. The follow through has involved well the occasional recalibration. Kutim aimed for the whole galaxy in a single launch. Server by contrast is proceeding one careful target at a time. Model language interface adoption. The industry is discovering that ambition makes headlines but actual delivery keeps the headlines relevant. It is not flawless not yet. Indis remains in limited beta with weightless and of course at times slower responses in its more advanced reasoning mode. Compute capacity is still a constraint. It feels well in some ways like the early days of the internet. Promising but uneven. Even so the direction however is clear. India no longer seems content to be simply a market for technology. It actually wants a hand in building it. And here of course as always is the front page take. As global companies compete for India's attention, a different ambition is coming to life to build at home and on home terms. Indis is more than a product launch. It voices intent. Technology shaped by local languages, local realities, and local priorities. And of course, encouragingly, the conversation is only beginning. Oh, and by the way, if you've tried Indis, please share your experience with us. We're definitely listening. This, ladies and gentlemen, is front page by the A IM network. Like, share, subscribe, and always remember, think AI, think AIM.

title: Sarvam Indus vs OpenAI: Inside India’s Sovereign ChatGPT Rival
